title: Not all data is the same
instruction:
In Chapter 2, you stored your name and your age in variables. But did you notice? Your name had quotes around it and your age did not.

That is not random. Python treats different kinds of data differently — and it calls those kinds **types**.

`type()` tells you what type a value is. Run this and see what comes back.
successMessage: Four values, four types: str (text), int (whole number), float (decimal), bool (yes/no). Python figured all of that out automatically from what you wrote. No declarations, no setup.

title: str — anything you can read
instruction:
A `str` (short for 'string') holds text. Your name is a string. An email is a string. A tweet is a string.

Strings need quotes. Single `'hello'` or double `"hello"` — both work the same way in Python. Your call.

Create a variable `email` with your email and a variable `greeting` with a welcome message. Then print both.
hint:
email = "alex@example.com"
greeting = "Welcome to Python!"
print(email)
print(greeting)
starter: # TODO: create email and greeting variables, then print them

codeChecks.message: Create an email variable with quotes around the value.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Strings all around us. Names, emails, messages, addresses, URLs — if it's readable text, it's a string.

title: int — whole numbers
instruction:
An `int` is a whole number. No decimal point. Positive, negative, or zero.

`42`, `-7`, `0`, `1_000_000` — that last one uses underscores for readability. Python just ignores them. Try it!

Create `year`, `population`, and `temperature` (make temperature negative — brrr). Print all three.
starter: # TODO: create year, population, and temperature (make it negative!) variables

codeChecks.message: Create year as a 4-digit integer (no quotes).
Create temperature as a negative integer (start with -).
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Ints are for counting. Ages, years, quantities, scores, rankings. When a value is always a whole number, int is the right choice.

title: float — when you need more precision
instruction:
A `float` is a number with a decimal point: `3.14`, `99.99`, `-0.5`.

Use floats for prices, temperatures, percentages, distances — anything that needs precision beyond a whole number.

Create `price` as 99.99, `pi` as 3.14159, and `percentage` as 87.5. Print all three.
starter: # TODO: create price, pi, and percentage as floats (with decimal points)

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Floats for decimals, ints for whole numbers. One rule: 10 / 3 gives you a float (3.333...), 10 // 3 gives you an int (3). Python has different operators for each!

title: bool — the yes/no type
instruction:
A `bool` can only be one of two values: `True` or `False`.

That's it. Two options. And yet booleans control pretty much everything — is the user logged in? Did the payment succeed? Is the list empty?

Notice: capital T and F. `true` (lowercase) is not a Python boolean — it will crash.

Create `is_logged_in = True`, `is_premium = False`, `has_errors = False`. Print all three.
hint:
is_logged_in = True
is_premium = False
has_errors = False
starter: # TODO: create three boolean variables and print them

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Every if-statement, every decision, every loop condition — they all come down to True or False. Booleans are the bedrock of all program logic.

title: None — the 'nothing here yet' type
instruction:
Sometimes you need to say 'this variable exists, but has no value yet'. That is what `None` is for.

None is not zero. It is not an empty string. It is the deliberate absence of a value.

Think of it like an empty box with a label on it — the box exists, but there is nothing inside.

Run this, then add `result = None` and print it.
starter:
user = None
print(user)
print(type(user))
# TODO: add result = None and print it

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: None is everywhere. Functions that don't return anything return None. Missing API fields are None. Uninitialised values are None. You will see it constantly.

title: Python figures out the type for you
instruction:
Here is something wild: you never have to tell Python what type a variable is. Python infers it from the value.

`x = 42` — Python makes it an int automatically.
`x = "hello"` — Python makes it a string automatically.

This is called **dynamic typing** and it's one of the reasons Python is so fast to write.

Run this and see all five types Python inferred.
starter:
a = 100
b = 3.14
c = "Python"
d = True
e = None

print(type(a).__name__)
print(type(b).__name__)
print(type(c).__name__)
print(type(d).__name__)
print(type(e).__name__)
successMessage: Python inferred all five types without you saying a word. int, float, str, bool, NoneType — all from the values alone. No declarations, no type keywords. Pure Python magic.

title: The sneaky "42" vs 42 trap
instruction:
This is the most common type mistake beginners make. Seriously, everyone does this at least once.

`"42"` is text that looks like a number. `42` is an actual number.

They look similar but behave completely differently. Watch what the `+` operator does to each one.
starter:
a = "42"
b = "8"
print(a + b)

c = 42
d = 8
print(c + d)
successMessage: "42" + "8" = "428" (they got glued together as text). 42 + 8 = 50 (actual math). Same operator, totally different result. This trips up every beginner. And now you understand exactly why.

title: int(), float(), str() — convert between types
instruction:
You can convert between types:
- `int("25")` → turns the string "25" into the number 25
- `float(25)` → turns 25 into 25.0
- `str(25)` → turns 25 into the string "25"

The code below is broken — it tries to add 1 to the string `"25"`. Convert `age` to int first, then add 1.

Remember: when you read input from a user, it always comes in as a string. Converting to int is something you will do constantly.
starter:
age = "25"
# BUG: can't add 1 to a string — convert age to int first
age = age + 1
print(age)

codeChecks.message: Convert age to int first: age = int(age)

successMessage: int() converted the text "25" to the number 25, then + 1 worked perfectly. You will do this conversion constantly — user input is always a string.

title: str() — join text with numbers
instruction:
You cannot glue a string and a number together directly:

`"Score: " + 99` → crashes with a TypeError

You need to convert the number to text first: `"Score: " + str(99)`

Fix the three broken lines below. (Heads up: in the next chapter you will learn f-strings, which make this way cleaner — but str() is good to know.)
starter:
score = 99
year = 2025
pi = 3.14

# BUG: these crash because you can't add strings and numbers — wrap the numbers in str()
print("Score: " + score)
print("Year: " + year)
print("Pi: " + pi)
successMessage: str() wraps any value in quotes, making it safe to join with text. Though honestly — Chapter 4 has f-strings, which make this SO much cleaner. Sneak preview: f"Score: {score}" just works.

title: isinstance() — double-check a type
instruction:
`isinstance(value, type)` returns `True` if the value is of that type — `False` otherwise.

Great for validation: before doing math on a value, make sure it's actually a number.

Run the first two checks, then add two more: check if `score` is an int, and if `name` is a str.
starter:
price = 9.99
name = "Alex"
score = 100

print(isinstance(price, float))
print(isinstance(price, int))
# TODO: check if score is int and if name is str

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: isinstance gives you a clean True or False. Use it in if-statements to validate before processing — 'if isinstance(value, int): do math, else: tell the user it's wrong'.

title: All five types — your turn
instruction:
Final challenge! No starter code.

Create one variable of each type:
- A string (your name)
- An int (your age)
- A float (a price, like 12.99)
- A bool (whether you're enjoying Python so far — `True` hopefully)
- A None (a result that isn't ready yet)

Then print each one.
hint: Five variables, five print() calls. Mix them up!

codeChecks.message: Create a string variable (text in quotes).
Create a float variable (number with decimal).

successMessage: All five types — you have covered the complete foundation of Python data. Every program you will ever write uses combinations of these. Chapter 3 done, and you are flying.
